item.title: Welcome to the OpenEmber documentation site
item.description: This site is built with Docusaurus and explains what OpenEmber and EmberLite are, how the architecture is layered, how to build each repository, and how the ember CLI fits in.
item.content:encoded:
This site is built with Docusaurus and explains what OpenEmber and EmberLite are, how the architecture is layered, how to build each repository, and how the ember CLI fits in.

Under Docs you will find:

  • How OpenEmber relates to EmberLite
  • A high-level architecture overview
  • Build instructions for the main tree and EmberLite
  • Common ember commands and environment management (add / use / list)

The ecosystem is evolving quickly—if you spot gaps or want new topics, please open an issue or PR on openember-docs.

item.title: Why EmberLite?
item.description: Even with OpenEmber (C++-first), we still need EmberLite in pure C: industrial, medical, and aerospace projects often prioritize determinism, static memory, and certifiability over language features. C keeps call graphs and resource usage easier to reason about and aligns well with MISRA-style rules.
item.content:encoded:
Even with OpenEmber (C++-first), we still need EmberLite in pure C: industrial, medical, and aerospace projects often prioritize determinism, static memory, and certifiability over language features. C keeps call graphs and resource usage easier to reason about and aligns well with MISRA-style rules.

EmberLite is not meant to replace ROS2 or OpenEmber—it gives resource-constrained embedded Linux devices a lightweight node model and pub/sub messaging while staying on the same Kconfig + CMake and ember CLI workflow, so teams can switch repos without relearning tooling.

Read more in the Introduction.
